section.title: Quadratic forms and symmetric matrices
section.p: Linear equations gave us flat geometry. A line, a plane, and a hyperplane all come from first powers of the variables. The next layer of geometry comes from second powers: x^2,\qquad xy,\qquad y^2. This is where bowls, domes, ellipses, hyperbolas, and saddle points first appear.

subsection.title: From ax^2+bxy+cy^2 to matrices
subsection.p: Picture a shallow metal dish whose height above a flat table is q(x,y)=5x^2+4xy+2y^2. The cross term 4xy means the dish is not aligned with the x- and y-axes. It is tilted relative to the coordinate grid.

The expression can be written with a matrix:
q(x,y) \amp= \begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}5\amp 2\\2\amp 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}.
Check the multiplication: \begin{pmatrix}5\amp 2\\2\amp 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}5x+2y\\2x+2y\end{pmatrix}. Then
\begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}5x+2y\\2x+2y\end{pmatrix} \amp= x(5x+2y)+y(2x+2y)\\ \amp= 5x^2+4xy+2y^2.
The off-diagonal entries both contribute to the xy-term: 2xy+2xy=4xy.

statement.p: A quadratic form in two variables is a function q:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R of the form q(x,y)=ax^2+bxy+cy^2. It can be written as \boxed{q(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}a\amp b/2\\b/2\amp c\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}.}
A square matrix A is symmetric if A^T=A. For a 2\times2 matrix, this means A=\begin{pmatrix}a\amp b\\b\amp c\end{pmatrix}.

subsection.p:
Symmetric matrices are the natural matrices for quadratic forms. To see why, try a matrix that is not symmetric: M=\begin{pmatrix}5\amp 4\\0\amp 2\end{pmatrix}. Then
\begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}M\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix} \amp= \begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}5x+4y\\2y\end{pmatrix}\\ \amp= 5x^2+4xy+2y^2.
This is the same quadratic form as before. The nonsymmetric matrix M did not add a new quadratic form.
Its symmetric part is \frac12(M+M^T)=\frac12\left(\begin{pmatrix}5\amp 4\\0\amp 2\end{pmatrix}+\begin{pmatrix}5\amp 0\\4\amp 2\end{pmatrix}\right)=\begin{pmatrix}5\amp 2\\2\amp 2\end{pmatrix}.

statement.p: For any square matrix M and any vector \mathbf x, \boxed{\mathbf x^T M\mathbf x=\mathbf x^T\left(\frac{M+M^T}{2}\right)\mathbf x.}

proof.p: Split M into two pieces: M=\frac{M+M^T}{2}+\frac{M-M^T}{2}. The first piece is symmetric. The second piece is skew-symmetric, meaning its transpose is its negative.

Let K=\frac{M-M^T}{2}. Then K^T=-K. The number \mathbf x^TK\mathbf x is a 1\times1 matrix, so it equals its own transpose: \mathbf x^TK\mathbf x=(\mathbf x^TK\mathbf x)^T=\mathbf x^TK^T\mathbf x=-\mathbf x^TK\mathbf x. Therefore \mathbf x^TK\mathbf x=0. So only the symmetric part contributes.

subsection.p: The hypothesis that we use the symmetric part is not cosmetic. For example, K=\begin{pmatrix}0\amp 10\\-10\amp 0\end{pmatrix} has determinant 100, but \begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}K\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}10y\\-10x\end{pmatrix}=10xy-10xy=0 for every (x,y). As a linear transformation, K rotates and scales. As a quadratic form, it contributes nothing. That is why quadratic forms belong to symmetric matrices.

Despite the name, a quadratic form is not the same thing as a differential form. A quadratic form takes one vector and returns a number that scales like the square of the input: q(c\mathbf v)=c^2q(\mathbf v). Differential forms later will be linear, alternating measuring rules used as integrands. The shared word form means a rule with algebraic structure, but the structures are different.

subsection.title: Ellipses, hyperbolas, and rotated axes
subsection.p:
The quadratic form q(x,y)=5x^2+4xy+2y^2 is always positive except at the origin. One way to see this is to complete the square:
q(x,y) \amp= 5x^2+4xy+2y^2\\ \amp= 5\left(x+\frac25y\right)^2+\frac65y^2.
Both terms are nonnegative, and both are zero only when x=0,\qquad y=0. So the graph z=q(x,y) is a bowl.
The level curves q(x,y)=c for c>0 are ellipses. But they are rotated ellipses, because of the cross term 4xy.

A better coordinate system reveals the shape. Let \mathbf p=\frac1{\sqrt5}(2,1),\qquad \mathbf r=\frac1{\sqrt5}(1,-2). These are perpendicular unit vectors. Use them as new axes: (x,y)=u\mathbf p+v\mathbf r. That means x=\frac{2u+v}{\sqrt5},\qquad y=\frac{u-2v}{\sqrt5}. Substituting into q gives \boxed{q(x,y)=6u^2+v^2.} In the rotated coordinates, the cross term disappears. The level curve q(x,y)=1 becomes 6u^2+v^2=1, which is an ellipse aligned with the u- and v-axes.

figure.caption: The cross term 4xy rotates the ellipse. In the right axes, it becomes 6u^2+v^2=1.

subsection.p:
The same idea explains hyperbolas. Consider q(x,y)=2xy. This has only a cross term. Use the rotated coordinates u=\frac{x+y}{\sqrt2},\qquad v=\frac{x-y}{\sqrt2}. Solving for x and y, x=\frac{u+v}{\sqrt2},\qquad y=\frac{u-v}{\sqrt2}. Then
2xy \amp= 2\left(\frac{u+v}{\sqrt2}\right)\left(\frac{u-v}{\sqrt2}\right)\\ \amp= u^2-v^2.
So 2xy=1 is the rotated version of u^2-v^2=1, a hyperbola.
The graph z=2xy is a saddle. Along the line y=x, z=2x^2, so the surface curves upward. Along the line y=-x, z=-2x^2, so the surface curves downward.
The same point is both valley-like and ridge-like, depending on the direction.

subsection.title: Positive definite, negative definite, and indefinite forms
subsection.p: A quadratic form can have different sign patterns.

statement.p: Let q(\mathbf x)=\mathbf x^TA\mathbf x be a quadratic form.

row.cell: if q(\mathbf x)>0 for every \mathbf x\neq\mathbf 0.
if q(\mathbf x)<0 for every \mathbf x\neq\mathbf 0.
if q takes both positive and negative values.

subsection.p: The pictures are memorable:

table.title: Sign patterns of a quadratic form and the shape of its graph.

subsection.p: There is also a borderline case. The form q(x,y)=(2x+y)^2 is never negative, but it is zero along the whole line 2x+y=0. So it is not positive definite. It has a flat direction.
For 2\times2 symmetric matrices, there is a simple determinant test.

statement.p: Let A=\begin{pmatrix}a\amp b\\b\amp c\end{pmatrix} be symmetric, and let q(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix}x\amp y\end{pmatrix}A\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=ax^2+2bxy+cy^2. Let \Delta=\det A=ac-b^2. Then:

row.cell: q is positive definite if and only if a>0 and \Delta>0.
q is negative definite if and only if a<0 and \Delta>0.

proof.p:
Assume first that a\neq0. Complete the square:
q(x,y) \amp= ax^2+2bxy+cy^2\\ \amp= a\left(x+\frac ba y\right)^2+\left(c-\frac{b^2}{a}\right)y^2\\ \amp= a\left(x+\frac ba y\right)^2+\frac{ac-b^2}{a}y^2\\ \amp= a\left(x+\frac ba y\right)^2+\frac{\Delta}{a}y^2.
If a>0 and \Delta>0, then both coefficients are positive. The form is positive except at the origin.
If a<0 and \Delta>0, then both coefficients are negative. The form is negative except at the origin.
If \Delta<0 and a\neq0, then the two square terms have opposite signs. The form takes both positive and negative values, so it is indefinite.
If a=0 and \Delta<0, then \Delta=-b^2<0, so b\neq0. In that case q(x,y)=2bxy+cy^2. Taking y=1, the expression q(x,1)=2bx+c takes both positive and negative values as x varies. So q is indefinite.

subsection.p: Each condition matters.

First, \Delta>0 alone does not mean positive definite. The matrix A=\begin{pmatrix}-1\amp 0\\0\amp -1\end{pmatrix} has \det A=1>0, but q(x,y)=-x^2-y^2 is negative definite, not positive definite. We also need the sign of a.

Second, a>0 alone does not mean positive definite. The matrix A=\begin{pmatrix}1\amp 0\\0\amp -1\end{pmatrix} has a=1>0, but q(x,y)=x^2-y^2 takes both signs. The determinant is \det A=-1<0, and the form is indefinite.

Third, when \Delta=0, there is a flat direction. For example, q(x,y)=(2x+y)^2 has matrix A=\begin{pmatrix}4\amp 2\\2\amp 1\end{pmatrix}, and \det A=4\cdot1-2\cdot2=0. The form is not positive definite because q(1,-2)=0 even though (1,-2)\neq(0,0).
The determinant has returned as a collapse detector. For a linear transformation, \det A=0 meant area was collapsed. For a quadratic form, \det A=0 means one curvature direction has flattened.

subsection.title: Preview: the Hessian matrix will classify critical points
subsection.p: Quadratic forms matter in calculus because a curved function often looks quadratic after its linear part disappears.

Suppose a hiker stands at a point on a hill where the tangent plane is horizontal. There is no first-order uphill direction at that exact point. The next question is not Which way is the slope? The slope starts at zero. The next question is: \text{Does the surface bend up, bend down, or bend both ways?} That bending is measured by a quadratic form.

Later, near a point (a,b), a function f(x,y) will have an approximation of the form f(a+h,b+k)\approx f(a,b)+\text{linear part}+\frac12\begin{pmatrix}h\amp k\end{pmatrix}H\begin{pmatrix}h\\k\end{pmatrix}. The matrix H=\begin{pmatrix}f_{xx}\amp f_{xy}\\f_{yx}\amp f_{yy}\end{pmatrix} is called the Hessian matrix.
At a critical point, the linear part is zero. Then the Hessian's quadratic form becomes the first visible shape:

table.title: The Hessian's sign pattern and the local shape at a critical point.

row.cell: the quadratic part is not enough

subsection.p: This is only a preview. We do not yet have the derivative of a multivariable function. But the pattern is now visible: matrices describe the best linear part, and symmetric matrices describe the first curved part. The next step is to make the phrase best linear part precise. That phrase will become the multivariable derivative.
